title: 9 Nehase — St. Or of Nitria & The Assembly
saints: St. Or of Nitria, Desert Father
narrative:
On the ninth day of Nehase, the third day of Filseta, the Church honors Abba Or of Nitria, one of the great luminaries of the Egyptian desert in its golden age. Or was among those remarkable Abbas who had received so little formal education and yet became a treasury of wisdom so profound that visitors traveled from the ends of the Roman world to sit at his feet. The desert fathers understood the fast not as a technique for self-improvement but as a form of warfare — a combat against the passions in which the body's hunger is converted into the soul's alertness, and the time normally spent eating and drinking is given back to God in prayer. Abba Or lived this understanding in its full severity and its full joy, and those who came to him found a man who had been emptied of self in order to be filled with something else entirely. On this third day of Filseta, his witness calls the fasting community to go deeper, to trust the emptiness.

The monthly commemoration of St. Matthias the Apostle fills today's assembly with the specific grace of divine election and perseverance. Matthias was not one of the Twelve from the beginning — he was chosen by lot, after the Resurrection, to fill the place left vacant by Judas. His election is, in the language of theology, a consolation for all those who come late, who were not present at the beginning, who feel themselves to be supplements rather than originals. Matthias was there from the Baptism of John through the Resurrection; he had witnessed everything, prepared in obscurity, and waited. When the lot fell on him he did not protest his unworthiness. He entered the apostolic college and tradition assigns him a mission to Georgia and Ethiopia — to this very region — which makes his monthly commemoration on the Ethiopian calendar more than ceremonial. He may have walked on the soil that his spiritual children now fast upon.

St. Julian of Constantinople, martyr and witness to the faith in the great imperial city, brings the urban world of courts and commerce into this desert assembly. Julian suffered for Christ in the context of the city — not in the solitude of Nitria but in the public density of Constantinople — and his witness reminds the Filseta community that the ascetic discipline they are practicing in their homes and churches is a form of the same readiness that Julian displayed in the imperial city. The fast is the practice; the martyr's witness is the examination. Those who have learned to say no to their appetites in the fast have been trained, however imperfectly, for the moment when they must say no to something far more demanding.

Or, Matthias, and Julian together on this ninth day of Nehase present a map of holy geography: the desert, the apostolic road, and the city. The Filseta fast does not exempt anyone from their particular location in the world — it enters each location and transforms it. The solitary fasts in his cell; the householder fasts in his home; the citizen fasts in his city. What unites them all is the common intention: to become more capable of receiving the grace that will be poured out in fullness on the day of the Assumption, when heaven and earth embrace once more in the person of the one who bore the Lord.
